works.title: Wise

He was not only a formidable military commander but also a ruler who governed the state with profound deliberation.

works.title: Life and mission
works.desc: Al-Biruni (973–1048) — one of the most universally gifted intellects of the Islamic Golden Age. This Turkic scholar from Khwarazm (present-day Uzbekistan) mastered mathematics, astronomy, physics, geography, history, pharmacology, and linguistics with equal brilliance.

works.title: Direction of action
works.desc: Al-Biruni was born in 973 in the Birun (external) quarter of the city of Khwarazm, that's why he got the name 'Biruni' (outer quarter). Of Turkic origin, the scholar grew up at the court of the Khwarazmshahs and from an early age immersed himself deeply in mathematics, astronomy, and philosophy.

works.title: Direction of action
works.desc: Throughout his life, Biruni operated under difficult political conditions. The Ghaznavid ruler Sultan Mahmud took him to his court by force and kept him there for thirteen years. Yet Biruni turned this forced sojourn to advantage — accompanying military expeditions to Afghanistan and India, he studied India first-hand.

works.title: Life and mission
works.desc: Ahmad Yasawi (1093–1166) — the greatest Sufi poet and spiritual leader of the Turkic world. This revered figure from Yasi (present-day Turkistan, Kazakhstan) is the founder of the spiritual movement that spread Islam among the Turkic peoples of Central Asia.

works.title: Direction of action
works.desc: Ahmad Yasawi was born in 1093 in the city of Yasi (present-day Turkestan, Kazakhstan). Having lost his father at an early age, he traveled to Bukhara to study under Yusuf Hamadani, one of the great Sufi masters of his time. After his master's death, he returned to Yasi, where he established a large spiritual community.

works.title: Direction of action
works.desc: When Yasawi reached the age of 63 — the age at which the Prophet passed away — he declared himself unworthy of living among the people and retreated to an underground cell, a form of subterranean hermitage. He spent the last thirty years of his life there, yet never severed his connection with the people.

works.desc: Al-Farabi — full name Abu Nasr Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Farabi — was born around 872 near the city of Farab. This city is located in the Turkestan region of present-day Kazakhstan. works.title: The second teacher

works.title: Direction of action

works.title: Life and mission
works.desc: Kul Tigin (684–731) — the greatest military commander of the Second Gokturk Khaganate and the right hand of his brother Bilge Khagan. A hero who concluded every battle in victory and devoted his life to the freedom of the Turkic people.

works.title: Direction of action
works.desc: Kul Tigin was born in 684 as the son of Ilterish Khagan and is recognized as one of the greatest military geniuses in Turkic history. He grew up on battlefields from an early age; his mastery on horseback, his courage, and his swiftness turned him into a legendary figure.

works.title: Direction of action
works.desc: He complemented his brother Bilge Khagan's political governance with his military strength. In all the great battles fought by the Turks against the Chinese, the Tatabi, the Turgesh, and the Kyrgyz, Kul Tigin stood at the forefront. In one battle, five horses were killed under him, yet he refused to drop his sword despite his wounds.

works.title: Divanu Dictionary-it Turk
works.desc: Mahmud al-Kashgari was a linguist, geographer, and encyclopedist of Karakhanid Turkic origin. He completed Diwan Lughat al-Turk (The Compendium of Turkic Languages) in Baghdad between 1072 and 1074, dedicating it to the Abbasid Caliph al-Muqtadi Billah. His aim was to introduce the Turkic language to the Baghdad court and teach Arabic speakers Turkic.

works.title: Content of the work
works.desc: For this purpose, he first traveled all the Turkic lands — personally visiting every region inhabited by Turkic tribes, from Kashgar onwards, to collect linguistic specimens. The collected material is so rich that many Turkic dialects no longer extant today can only be known through Kashgari's work.

works.title: Life and mission
works.desc: Omar Khayyam (1048–1131) — a rare genius who united mathematics, astronomy, philosophy, and poetry in a single life. As a Khorasani Turkic scholar, he entered the history of mathematics through his work on cubic equations, participated in the creation of the Jalali calendar, yet became known to the world through the melancholy philosophy of his rubaiyat.

works.title: Khayyam
works.desc: Omar Khayyam was born in 1048 in the city of Nishapur. He is believed to have been of Turkic origin; the name 'Khayyam' alludes to descent from tentmakers. From youth he attracted attention through talent in mathematics, astronomy, and philosophy, and worked at the Seljuk court.

works.title: Direction of action
works.desc: In mathematics, he solved cubic equations by geometrical methods — five hundred years before Europeans solved the same problems. He served as chief astronomer in the preparation of the Jalali calendar — a calendar more accurate than the Gregorian.

works.title: Good knowledge

The khan rewarded him by conferring the title of 'Has Hajib' (Special Court Chamberlain).
